edges.description: Abnormally assembled type I collagen with poorly secreted, degradation-prone procollagen molecules impairs extracellular matrix formation and fibril organization, producing structurally weak bone.
edges.target: Defective Bone Matrix and Progressive Bone Fragility

edges.description: Repeated fractures and impaired bone remodeling progressively deform the long bones and spine, leading to scoliosis, thoracic cage deformity, and consequent respiratory compromise.
intermediate_mechanisms: recurrent fractures and impaired bone remodeling
progressive long-bone bowing and spinal curvature
edges.source: Defective Bone Matrix and Progressive Bone Fragility
edges.target: Progressive Skeletal Deformity and Thoracic Compromise

nodes.description: Skull-base deformity with upward displacement of the posterior fossa floor occurs in severe OI and is enriched in types III and IV.

evidence.reference_title: Skull base abnormalities in osteogenesis imperfecta: a cephalometric evaluation of 54 patients and 108 control volunteers.
evidence.snippet: The normal mean distances were exceeded by more than two standard deviations (SDs) in 28.3 to 35.2%, and by more than three SDs in 13.2 to 16.6% of the patients with OI. The latter figures reliably reflect the prevalence of basilar impression.

nodes.description: Blue scleral hue can occur in type III OI, but it may be subtler than in type I and can lessen with age.

evidence.reference_title: Genetic heterogeneity in osteogenesis imperfecta.
evidence.snippet: The density of scleral blueness appeared less than that seen in the first group of patients and approximated that seen in normal children and adults. Moreover, the blueness appeared to decrease with age.

evidence.reference_title: Histopathologic and electron-microscopic features of corneal and scleral collagen fibers in osteogenesis imperfecta type III.
evidence.snippet: The sclera had a blue color and was moderately thinned, especially at the equator.

nodes.description: Chronic bone pain is a prominent feature in type III OI, driven by repeated fractures, deformity, and the underlying connective tissue disorder.

evidence.snippet: HP:0002653 | Bone pain | Frequent (79-30%)

evidence.reference_title: Incorporating the patient perspective in the study of rare bone disease.
evidence.snippet: Other areas included pain, gastrointestinal problems,

nodes.description: Abnormally assembled type I collagen fibrils reduce extracellular matrix integrity and impair mineralization, producing bone that is brittle and structurally weak. Osteoblasts incorporating mutant procollagen molecules secrete collagen fibres that are abnormally organised and where mineral deposition is impaired. Markedly reduced bone mineral density for age and thinned cortices underlie the severe bone fragility that manifests as recurrent fractures beginning at or near birth in type III OI.
nodes.id: Defective Bone Matrix and Progressive Bone Fragility

evidence.reference_title: Perinatal lethal osteogenesis imperfecta.
evidence.snippet: The collagen fibres are abnormally organised and mineralisation is impaired.

evidence.reference_title: Perinatal lethal osteogenesis imperfecta.
evidence.snippet: The abnormal molecules are poorly secreted, more susceptible to degradation, and impair the formation of the extracellular matrix.

nodes.description: Structural dentin abnormality with discoloration and fragility is a recurrent dental manifestation in type III OI.

evidence.reference_title: Osteogenesis imperfecta: a clinical study of the first ten years of life.
evidence.snippet: Only fracture nonunion, dentinogenesis imperfecta, and congenital cardiac malformations were more frequent in type III than in type IV.

evidence.reference_title: Osteogenesis imperfecta and the teeth, eyes, and ears-a study of non-skeletal phenotypes in adults.
evidence.snippet: Dentinogenesis imperfecta was diagnosed in one fourth of the patients, based on clinical and radiographic findings. This condition was predominately seen in patients with moderate to severe OI.

nodes.description: Structural mutations in COL1A1 or COL1A2, typically glycine substitutions in the triple helical domain, produce abnormal collagen that disrupts fibril assembly. The mutations are generally less severe than those causing type II, allowing survival but with progressive deformity.

evidence.reference_title: Perinatal lethal osteogenesis imperfecta.
evidence.snippet: Point mutations resulting in the substitution of Gly residues in Gly-X-Y amino acid triplets of the triple helical domain of the alpha 1(I) or alpha 2(I) chains are the most frequent mutations. They interrupt the repetitive Gly-X-Y structure that is mandatory for the formation of a stable triple helix.

nodes.description: Bowing of the femur from repeated fractures and impaired bone remodeling is a prominent long-bone deformity in type III OI, often requiring intramedullary rodding with osteotomies to correct.

evidence.snippet: HP:0002980 | Femoral bowing | Frequent (79-30%)

evidence.reference_title: Surgical Strategy to Decrease the Revision Rate of Fassier-Duval Nailing in the Lower Limbs of Osteogenesis Imperfecta.
evidence.snippet: osteotomy to correct bowing are the key factors when using the FD nail.

nodes.description: Hearing loss can occur in childhood in type III OI and may be conductive, sensorineural, or mixed.

evidence.reference_title: Hearing loss in individuals with osteogenesis imperfecta in North America: Results from a multicenter study.
evidence.snippet: Individuals with OI Types III and IV are at a higher risk to develop HL in the first decade of life when compared to OI Type I.

evidence.reference_title: Osteogenesis imperfecta and the teeth, eyes, and ears-a study of non-skeletal phenotypes in adults.
evidence.snippet: The most prevalent type of hearing loss (HL) was sensorineural hearing loss, whereas conductive HL was solely seen in patients with OI type III.

nodes.description: Kyphosis is a common spinal deformity in type III OI, often occurring together with scoliosis as multilevel spine deformity.

evidence.snippet: HP:0002808 | Kyphosis | Occasional (29-5%)

nodes.description: Most adults with OI type III lose independent ambulation and rely on wheelchair mobility, reflecting the severity of bone fragility and progressive deformity.

evidence.snippet: HP:0002505 | Loss of ambulation | Frequent (79-30%)

evidence.reference_title: Osteogenesis imperfecta: profiles of motor development as assessed by a postal questionnaire.
evidence.snippet: In type III, the development of all motor milestones was

nodes.description: Pectus carinatum (pigeon chest deformity) results from repeated rib fractures and impaired thoracic cage development in type III OI.

evidence.snippet: HP:0000768 | Pectus carinatum | Very frequent (99-80%)

nodes.description: Metaphyseal and epiphyseal "popcorn" calcifications around the growth plate usually emerge in childhood and most often involve the distal femora and proximal tibiae.

evidence.reference_title: Popcorn calcification in osteogenesis imperfecta: incidence, progression, and molecular correlation.
evidence.snippet: Popcorn calcifications were present in 13 of 25 type III (52%), but only 2 of 20 type IV (10%), OI children. The mean age of onset was 7.0 years, with a range of 4-14 years.

nodes.description: Repeated fractures and impaired bone remodeling progressively deform the long bones and spine in type III OI. Virtually all patients develop scoliosis; in one cohort, scoliosis was present in all 42 patients evaluated, with a mean thoracic curve of 66°. Increasing thoracic scoliosis compresses thoracic volume and reduces chest-wall compliance, correlating with decreased vital lung capacity. Pulmonary impairment in type III OI has a combined etiology: scoliosis-related thoracic restriction and an intrinsic component related to the underlying connective-tissue abnormality.
nodes.id: Progressive Skeletal Deformity and Thoracic Compromise

evidence.reference_title: Prevalence of scoliosis and impaired pulmonary function in patients with type III osteogenesis imperfecta.
evidence.snippet: Increasing thoracic scoliosis was correlated with decreased vital lung capacity in our study population of type III OI patients.

evidence.reference_title: Prevalence of scoliosis and impaired pulmonary function in patients with type III osteogenesis imperfecta.
evidence.snippet: it is plausible that the pulmonary impairment found in type III OI patients is a combined issue, partly associated to scoliosis and partly intrinsic to OI.

nodes.description: Marked bone fragility causes repeated fractures, often beginning at or around birth in the severe type III phenotype.

evidence.reference_title: Genetic heterogeneity in osteogenesis imperfecta.
evidence.snippet: A third group, two thirds of whom had fractures at birth, showed severe progressive deformity of limbs and spine.

evidence.reference_title: Growth characteristics in individuals with osteogenesis imperfecta in North America: results from a multicenter study.
evidence.snippet: PURPOSE: Osteogenesis imperfecta (OI) predisposes people to recurrent fractures, bone deformities, and short stature.

nodes.description: Markedly reduced bone mineral density for age underlies the extreme bone fragility of type III OI.

evidence.snippet: HP:0004349 | Reduced bone mineral density | Frequent (79-30%)

evidence.reference_title: Growth characteristics in individuals with osteogenesis imperfecta in North America: results from a multicenter study.
evidence.snippet: PURPOSE: Osteogenesis imperfecta (OI) predisposes people to recurrent fractures,

nodes.description: Pulmonary impairment in type III OI shows a restrictive pattern and worsens with increasing thoracic scoliosis.

evidence.reference_title: Prevalence of scoliosis and impaired pulmonary function in patients with type III osteogenesis imperfecta.
evidence.snippet: Restrictive lung pathophysiology was shown in our study population with a mean FEV1/FVC ratio of 0.85.

nodes.description: Progressive spinal curvature is a major source of morbidity in type III OI.

evidence.reference_title: Behavior of scoliosis during growth in children with osteogenesis imperfecta.
evidence.snippet: Scoliosis prevalence (68%) and mean progression rate (6° per year) were the highest in the group of patients with the most severe osteogenesis imperfecta (modified Sillence type III).

evidence.reference_title: Prevalence of scoliosis and impaired pulmonary function in patients with type III osteogenesis imperfecta.
evidence.snippet: All 42 patients had scoliosis, with a mean curve of 66° (95% CI of range).

nodes.description: Marked growth failure is a core manifestation of type III OI, with very low childhood height z-scores and diminished growth velocity.

evidence.reference_title: Growth characteristics in individuals with osteogenesis imperfecta in North America: results from a multicenter study.
evidence.snippet: In children, the median z-scores for height in OI types I, III, and IV were -0.66, -6.91, and -2.79, respectively. Growth velocity was diminished in OI types III and IV.

evidence.reference_title: Osteogenesis imperfecta: a clinical study of the first ten years of life.
evidence.snippet: Although types III and IV patients suffered from severe short stature, serum insulin-like growth factor (IGF) I was in the normal range.

nodes.description: Triangular facial shape has been reported as part of the craniofacial phenotype of type III OI.

evidence.reference_title: Osteogenesis imperfecta type III: Oral, craniofacial characteristics and atypical radiographic findings oral.
evidence.snippet: An eighteen-year-old, male patient diagnosed with osteogenesis imperfecta type III was referred for dental evaluation; the clinical examination showed the craniofacial and oral changes of the disease such as triangular face, class III malocclusion, anterior open bite and posterior crossbite, dentinogenesis imperfecta presenting amber discoloration.

nodes.description: Accessory sutural bones are a recurrent cranial radiographic finding in the more severe type III/IV forms of OI.

evidence.reference_title: Skull base abnormalities in osteogenesis imperfecta: a cephalometric evaluation of 54 patients and 108 control volunteers.
evidence.snippet: These three abnormalities and wormian bones were predominantly found in OI Types III and IV as well as in patients exhibiting dentinal abnormality.
